headline: BEANS BOUNCE, CATTLE DRIFTS AS HORMUZ DEAL EASES
subheadline: Soybeans lead grains higher on fund rotation while livestock marks time ahead of crop data.
lead: Soybeans ran 1.7% to $11.32, the biggest daily gain in three weeks, as funds rotated back into the complex ahead of Tuesday's USDA Crop Progress report. Live cattle drifted 0.6% to $241.18, holding above the $240 floor but lacking conviction. The Hormuz deal that cleared the first LNG tanker Monday morning is pulling energy premium out of input costs, but beans are acting like the funds finally found their footing.
the_takeaway: Beans leading, cattle waiting, Tuesday's crop data decides which story runs.
teaser: Fund rotation drives beans higher while cattle holds pattern ahead of crop data.

one_number.unit: feeder cattle to live cattle ratio
one_number.context: Feeders at $357.42 divided by live at $241.18 gives a 1.47 ratio, the tightest in two months. When this ratio compresses below 1.45, feeders historically outperform live cattle by 200 basis points over the next month. The cattle complex is setting up for feeder leadership, assuming the feed grain story stays cooperative.

yesterdays_call.summary: Corn found the floor, funds rotating in, ethanol story getting legs.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn only managed 0.3% today, beans took the rotation story instead.

sections.title: BEANS LEAD GRAINS HIGHER
sections.body: Soybeans gained 19 cents to $11.32, the **biggest single-day move in three weeks** as managed money rotated back into the complex. The November contract held steady at $11.32, keeping the carry structure intact while the front month caught up. Corn tagged along for a 3-cent gain to $4.13, but wheat gave back 5 cents to $5.84 on continued fund liquidation. **Beans are acting like the funds finally found conviction**, not just covering shorts.
sections.catalyst: Strait of Hormuz deal easing input cost pressures, fund rotation into beans
sections.bottom_line: Fund money flowing into beans, corn riding coattails, wheat still out of favor.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: took the fund rotation story that corn was supposed to own.

sections.title: CATTLE DRIFTS, FEEDERS TRACK
sections.body: Live cattle eased 0.6% to $241.18, marking time just above the $241 level that's been the pivot since early June. Feeders fell the same percentage to $357.42, keeping the ratio tight at 1.47, the **narrowest spread in two months**. No fresh news drove the drift; looks like position adjustment ahead of Tuesday's data calendar. The cattle complex is acting patient, not stepped out, but it needs a catalyst to break out of this 10-day range.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle holding pattern above key support, waiting for next catalyst.

sections.title: OATS FALL ON OVERNIGHT SELLING
sections.body: Oats tumbled 11¾ cents to $3.06, a **3.7% single-session decline** that wiped out last week's gains. The move started in overnight trade with no specific catalyst, looks like **fund liquidation** in a thin market. Oats have been the odd grain out all month, lacking the weather premium that's supported corn and beans. At $3.06, the contract is testing support that held in May, but the chart damage is real.
sections.bottom_line: Oats breaking lower on fund selling, thinnest grain getting hit hardest.

sections.title: ENERGY EASES ON HORMUZ PROGRESS
sections.body: Crude fell 1.6% to $84.88 as the **first LNG tanker cleared Hormuz since the U.S.-Iran deal** was announced. Natural gas bucked the trend, up 1.6% to $3.12, but the Hormuz premium that built since early April is deflating fast. The deal doesn't mean swift return to normal flows, but it removes the tail risk that had crude carrying a $8-12 geopolitical premium. Energy input costs for farmers are heading lower.
sections.catalyst: First LNG tanker clears Hormuz after U.S.-Iran agreement announcement
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz deal deflating energy premium, input costs easing for farmers.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA expands payment limits for farm program eligibility
outside_the_pit.body: Farm Service Agency is expanding payment limitation rules affecting program payments, allowing more equitable treatment of business entities. The changes matter for producers navigating ARC and PLC decisions.

outside_the_pit.title: El Niño could increase summer crop disease pressure
outside_the_pit.body: Syngenta specialist expects this summer's El Niño to create wetter conditions in the South, warmer temperatures up north. Disease pressure historically runs 15-20% above normal in El Niño summers.

outside_the_pit.title: Small meat processors getting $60M USDA support package
outside_the_pit.body: Agriculture Secretary Rollins launched the Small Processors Action Plan, targeting capacity constraints that have kept cattle basis wide since 2022. The money won't hit until 2027, but it's a structural positive.

spread_to_watch.label: November soybeans / July soybeans carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: November beans holding steady at $11.32 while July ran to the same level keeps the carry trade working. When this spread goes inverse, old crop is truly tight. Right now it's telling you there's still time to move beans, the market isn't panicking about summer weather yet.

basis.headline: Eastern Corn Belt basis firm on ethanol demand pickup
basis.body: Corn basis east of the Mississippi is **firming** as ethanol plants return from seasonal maintenance. The River is paying 10-15 cents over for June delivery, best premium since April. Western Belt staying soft, normal for the seasonal.

weekly_thread.question: Will Tuesday's crop progress show weather concerns or smooth sailing?
weekly_thread.status_text: The market's positioning defensively ahead of Tuesday's USDA Crop Progress report. Beans ran today on fund rotation, but if Tuesday shows stress in key growing regions, this could be the start of real weather premium pricing.

the_more_you_know.title: The feeder-live ratio: why 1.47 is the magic number
the_more_you_know.body: Today's feeder-to-live cattle ratio hit 1.47, the tightest in two months. When this ratio drops below 1.45, feeders historically outperform live cattle by an average 200 basis points over the next 30 days. The math is simple: tight ratios mean feeders are cheap relative to the end product, usually because feed costs are cooperating or because live cattle demand is stronger than expected. At 1.47, we're one tick away from the historical trigger level that says buy feeders, sell live cattle.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn above 85% good/excellent keeps weather premium out of prices
Soybean export sales: watch for Chinese bookings above 300K MT
Live cattle: $240 support either holds or breaks decisively

daily_quote.text: Patience is bitter, but its fruit is sweet.

source_summary: CBOT/CME settlement data, USDA crop progress calendar, Iran-Hormuz diplomatic updates, fund positioning analysis
